content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob have with them an array AAA of length NNN.

It is known that Ai=1A_i = 1Ai‚Äã=1 or Ai=‚àí1A_i = -1Ai‚Äã=‚àí1 for each 1‚â§i‚â§N1 \le i \le N1‚â§i‚â§N.

It is also guaranteed that there exists at least one index satisfying Ai=1A_i = 1Ai‚Äã=1.

They will play the following game on it:


On Alice's turn, she must choose two integers LLL and RRR such that 1‚â§L‚â§R‚â§‚à£A‚à£1 \le L \le R \le |A|1‚â§L‚â§R‚â§‚à£A‚à£ and

AL+AL+1+‚Ä¶+AR‚â•0A_L + A_{L+1} + \ldots + A_R \ge 0AL‚Äã+AL+1‚Äã+‚Ä¶+AR‚Äã‚â•0, and delete all the elements AL,AL+1,‚Ä¶,ARA_L, A_{L+1}, \ldots, A_RAL‚Äã,AL+1‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AR‚Äã from AAA.
On Bob's turn, he must choose two integers LLL and RRR such that 1‚â§L‚â§R‚â§‚à£A‚à£1 \le L \le R \le |A|1‚â§L‚â§R‚â§‚à£A‚à£ and

AL+AL+1+‚Ä¶+AR‚â§0A_L + A_{L+1} + \ldots + A_R \le 0AL‚Äã+AL+1‚Äã+‚Ä¶+AR‚Äã‚â§0, and delete all the elements AL,AL+1,‚Ä¶,ARA_L, A_{L+1}, \ldots, A_RAL‚Äã,AL+1‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AR‚Äã from AAA.
Here, ‚à£A‚à£|A|‚à£A‚à£ denotes the current length of AAA.

After each player's move, the remaining elements are re-indexed to start from 111, without changing their order.

For example, if A=[1,1,‚àí1,1,‚àí1]A = [1, 1, -1, 1, -1]A=[1,1,‚àí1,1,‚àí1], Alice can choose L=2,R=4L = 2, R = 4L=2,R=4 (which is valid because A2+A3+A4=1+(‚àí1)+1=1‚â•0A_2 + A_3 + A_4 = 1 + (-1) + 1 = 1 \ge 0A2‚Äã+A3‚Äã+A4‚Äã=1+(‚àí1)+1=1‚â•0) and delete the elements A2,A3,A4A_2, A_3, A_4A2‚Äã,A3‚Äã,A4‚Äã, after which the array becomes [1,‚àí1][1, -1][1,‚àí1].

The two take turns playing, with Alice moving first.

The first player who is **unable** to make a move **is the winner**. Note this unusual condition.

Given the array AAA, how many of Alice's first moves are winning?

That is, count the number of pairs (L,R)(L, R)(L,R) such that Alice can make her first move using this pair, and then has a strategy to win no matter what Bob does.

It is possible that Alice cannot win no matter what - in that case, output 000.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line contains a single integer NNN ‚Äî the length of the array AAA.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã ‚Äî the elements of the array.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: the number of Alice's first moves that are winning.
constraints: There exists an index iii with Ai=1A_i = 1Ai‚Äã=1.
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2‚ãÖ1052\cdot 10^52‚ãÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 222: A=[1,1,1]A = [1, 1, 1]A=[1,1,1].
Alice has no winning moves: whatever she does, Bob simply cannot make a move on his turn and automatically wins.
Test case 444: A=[‚àí1,1,‚àí1]A = [-1, 1, -1]A=[‚àí1,1,‚àí1]. Alice has three winning moves, namely:

L=1,R=2L = 1, R = 2L=1,R=2, for subarray [‚àí1,1][-1, 1][‚àí1,1] with sum 000.
This leaves Bob with the array [‚àí1][-1][‚àí1], and he's forced to delete the singular ‚àí1-1‚àí1.
The array is now empty on Alice's turn. Since she can't move, she wins.
L=2,R=3L = 2, R = 3L=2,R=3, for subarray [1,‚àí1][1, -1][1,‚àí1] with sum 000.
Again, this leaves Bob with [‚àí1][-1][‚àí1], and he's forced to take it so Alice wins.
L=2,R=2L = 2, R = 2L=2,R=2, for subarray [1][1][1] with sum 111.
This leaves Bob with [‚àí1,‚àí1][-1, -1][‚àí1,‚àí1], and no matter what Bob does, Alice won't be able to move on her turn and hence wins.
